question: State Cantor's Theorem in the context of set theory.
expectedPoints: A set cannot be put into one-to-one correspondence with its power set.
The power set of any set has a strictly greater cardinality than the set itself.
There is no surjection from a set onto its power set.

question: Outline the proof of Cantor's Theorem using a diagonalization argument.
expectedPoints: Assume, for contradiction, there exists a surjection f: S → P(S).
Construct the set D = {x ∈ S | x ∉ f(x)}.
Argue that D must be in the image of f, i.e., D = f(d) for some d ∈ S.
Show that this leads to the contradiction: d ∈ D if and only if d ∉ D.

question: Explain the significance of Cantor's Theorem and how it leads to the concept of an infinite hierarchy of infinities.
expectedPoints: It proves there are different 'sizes' or cardinalities of infinity.
Starting with the natural numbers, |N|, applying the theorem iteratively generates an infinite sequence of larger infinities: |N| < |P(N)| < |P(P(N))| < ...
This hierarchy shows that infinity is not a single concept and that some infinite sets are 'more infinite' than others.
The theorem is foundational for modern set theory and our understanding of the continuum.
